ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Chukka; mapped to closest parent class 'mutton dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mutton Chukka; A0CKM (cooked meat dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific South Indian mutton preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mutton Chukka is approximately 150 g, typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice, parotta, or roti in a South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Mutton Chukka are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked mutton (goat) composition data as the primary anchor, adjusted for the dry-roasted preparation style with spices and oil. Cholesterol value cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for cooked mutton dishes. Micronutrient values (phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are estimated from ICMR-NIN mutton composition tables and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked goat meat (NDB 17168) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Peer-reviewed analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Chukka in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the food's macronutrient profile: carbohydrate content is only 4.5 g per 100 g, with high protein (18.2 g) and fat (16.5 g) that substantially blunt glycemic response. Analogous reference: cooked meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content consistently yield GI values below 20 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 6.75) / 100 = 1.0 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from mutton and cooking oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mutton Chukka to occasional consumption, request reduced salt preparation, choose lean cuts, and minimize added oil to reduce both sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g) and high protein (18.2 g/100 g) with a GI of approximately 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Chukka is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (approximately 100–150 g) as its very low GI and high protein content support stable postprandial blood glucose; however, total calorie and saturated fat intake should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) from red meat, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Chukka sparingly, preferring lean mutton cuts, reduced oil, and lower-sodium preparation, as the saturated fat and cholesterol content may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

gout.key_factor: High purine content from red meat (mutton)
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or elevated uric acid should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Chukka, as the high purine content of mutton can precipitate acute gout attacks.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of red meat and high-heat dry-roasting
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Mutton Chukka in moderation; anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and black pepper present in the dish may partially offset the pro-inflammatory effect of red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Chukka, also known as Mutton Varuval, is a signature dry-style meat dish originating from the Madurai region of Tamil Nadu, South India. The word 'Chukka' means 'dry' in Tamil, describing the preparation technique in which bone-in or boneless mutton pieces are slow-cooked with a bold spice blend — including black peppercorns, fennel seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and coriander — until all moisture evaporates and the meat is coated in a thick, dark, intensely aromatic masala. The dish is finished by dry-roasting in a heavy pan with shallots, ginger-garlic paste, and curry leaves, producing a deeply caramelized, slightly crisp exterior with a tender, juicy interior. The flavor profile is intensely savory, peppery, and smoky, with a complex spice depth characteristic of Chettinad and Madurai culinary traditions. Nutritionally, Mutton Chukka is a high-protein (18.2 g/100 g), moderate-fat (16.5 g/100 g) dish with very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g), yielding approximately 240 kcal per 100 g. It is a rich source of zinc (4.2 mg), vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg), niacin (6.2 mg), and iron (2.8 mg), making it nutritionally dense for muscle maintenance and red blood cell support. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. However, its sodium content (380 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~6.2 g/100 g) warrant caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Mutton Chukka is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice, parotta, or roti, and is a staple at South Indian feasts and family meals.

meal_context: Protein-rich accompaniment to a rice meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked mutton composition as the primary anchor, with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked goat meat. No exact entry for Mutton Chukka exists in any single approved source; values reflect a defensible adaptation for the dry-roasted preparation style. GI is an analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA goat meat fatty acid profiles. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and water content are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this specific preparation are not available in approved sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Mutton Chukka in its dry-roasted preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN, Singapore HPB Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. Saturated fat (6.2 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy cooked goat meat fatty acid profiles. 3. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Chukka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Duplicate portion entries in the input JSON have been deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. 5. Halal status is flagged as true assuming standard preparation with halal-certified mutton; consumers should verify sourcing. 6. The input listed 'ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition' as the source, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
